text.title: Surveys Overview
text.summary: What UX surveys are, the four survey types, when to use and when not to use, and the six-step process from objective to analysis.

section.title: Definition
section.p: Surveys collect structured data from many users through standardized questions. They quantify user attitudes, preferences, and experiences — answering "what" and "how much" at scale, which qualitative methods cannot do efficiently.

list.item: Satisfaction: measure happiness with the product — CSAT, NPS, CES.
Task-based: post-task feedback after a specific flow — SUS, SEQ.
Preference: compare options — A vs B choice.
Exploratory: understand usage patterns and behaviors at scale.

rule.statement: Use surveys to quantify what you already know qualitatively, or to triage at scale. Do not use them to answer "why" questions — that requires interviews.
rule.rationale: Surveys give statistical confidence but no causal explanation. Using a survey to find out why users churn produces a list of surface symptoms, not root causes.

rule.statement: Do not launch a survey with fewer than 100 expected responses. With smaller samples, confidence intervals are too wide to act on.
rule.rationale: A sample of 30 produces a 95% CI of ±18 percentage points on a proportion. That range is wider than most meaningful differences you are trying to detect.

rule.statement: Place demographics at the end, not the start. Begin with low-effort, high-relevance questions to build respondent investment.
rule.rationale: Opening with demographics signals a corporate census rather than a conversation about the respondent's experience. Completion rates drop by 10-15% when demographics lead.

list.item: Step 1: define clear research objective — one primary question the survey must answer.
Step 2: choose question types (multiple choice, rating scale, ranking, open-ended, matrix).
Step 3: write questions — one concept per question, neutral wording, exhaustive options.
Step 4: design flow — intro, screening, easy questions, core questions, demographics, thank you.
Step 5: pilot test with 5-10 representative users before launch; fix confusion and measure completion time.
Step 6: analyze — compute response rate, run quantitative statistics, code open-ended themes, segment results.
